coredata.dc:title: Microstructural characterization of Nd2O3 in in situ synthesized multiple-reinforced (TiB+TiC+Nd2O3)/Ti composites
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Z.F.
Lu, W.J.
Qin, J.N.
Gu, J.J.
Zhang, D.
Liu, J.L.
coredata.dc:description:
In this work, the microstructures of Nd2O3 reinforcements in in situ synthesized (TiB+TiC+Nd2O3)/Ti composites were examined by back-scattered scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The interfacial bonding microstructure and orientation relationship between Nd2O3 and α-Ti was observed utilizing transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HREM). The orientation relationship of Nd2O3 and α-Ti can be described as:

Scanning and transmission electron microscopy
Orientation relationship

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM (bright field) image of Nd2O3 and surface scanning images of (b) Nd, (c) O and (d) Ti.
(a) TEM (bright field) picture of Nd2O3 particle and the selected area diffraction (SAD) patterns of (b) Nd2O3, (c) Ti and (d) Nd2O3–Ti interface.
Simulation SAD patterns of (a) Nd2O3, (b) α-Ti and (c) Nd2O3–α-Ti interface.
HREM image of the Nd2O3–Ti interface and its corresponding SAD patterns.
Fig. 5 Simulation SAD patterns of (a) Nd2O3, (b) α-Ti and (c) Nd2O3–α-Ti interface.
Magnified and optimized HREM images of (a) place A and (b) place B.

article.ce:title: Microstructural characterization of Nd2O3 in in situ synthesized multiple-reinforced (TiB+TiC+Nd2O3)/Ti composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Lab of Metal Matrix Composites, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, 1954 Huashan Road, Shanghai 200030, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Baosteel Research Institute, Baoshan Iron and Steel Co. Ltd., Shanghai 201900, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this work, the microstructures of Nd2O3 reinforcements in in situ synthesized (TiB+TiC+Nd2O3)/Ti composites were examined by back-scattered scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The interfacial bonding microstructure and orientation relationship between Nd2O3 and α-Ti was observed utilizing transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HREM). The orientation relationship of Nd2O3 and α-Ti can be described as:

ce:section.ce:para: Addition of rare earth elements in titanium alloys and titanium matrix composites (TMCs) has attracted much attention for increasing their strength and serve temperature [1–6]. Addition of rare earth elements has two main functions: one is to refine the matrix alloy and to increase its thermal-stability and oxygen resistance; the other is to decrease the oxygen content in the matrix resulting in formation of rare earth oxides (Re2O3) which are hard and thermal-stable ceramic particles and can function as reinforcements.

The features of reinforcements as well as the interfacial bonding characteristics play an important role in the composites. Extensive research works have been carried out on TiB, TiC, Al2O3, SiC, Re2O3 reinforcements and so on. In order to get good interfacial bonding, in situ technologies have been developed for synthesizing these reinforcements [7–15]. In situ synthesized reinforcements not only have clean reinforcement-matrix interface but also have certain orientation relationships between reinforcement and matrix or between various reinforcements [16–18], such as TiB–Ti, TiB–Nd2O3, TiB–Y2O3 and so on. When adding rare earth elements in synthesizing composites, rare earth oxides were in situ formed by nucleation and growth from parent matrix alloy during solidification, the interfacial contamination can be avoided, which helps to increase the mechanical properties.

In the previous work, the (TiB+TiC+Nd2O3)/Ti composites were successfully in situ synthesized utilizing the reaction between Ti, C, Nd and B2O3 through homogeneously melting in a non-consumable vacuum arc-remelting furnace [6]. In this work, the microstructure of Nd2O3 was examined and the interfacial bonding and orientation relationship between Nd2O3 and α-Ti was studied.

In synthesizing (TiB+TiC+Nd2O3)/Ti composites, the raw materials were grade II sponge titanium, C powder (99.8%, average particle size, 5–7μm), Nd metal (>99%) and B2O3 (>99%). The weight percentage of reactants and theoretical volume percentage of products were listed in Ref. [6]. Stoichiometric amounts of sponge titanium, B2O3, C and Nd were blended and melted in a non-consumable vacuum arc-remelting furnace. Considering the oxygen in the matrix, only 80% of calculated amount of B2O3 was added. The ingots were melted at least three times in order to ensure the chemical homogeneity of the composites.

JEOL JXA-8100 electron probe microanalyzer was used to examine the microstructures of Nd2O3 reinforcements. Samples for TEM and HREM experiments were prepared as follows: firstly, the samples were cut from the casting ingot. Secondly, the samples were thinned to 100μm by polishing with SiC paper. Thirdly, the samples were punched to discs with a diameter of 3.0mm. Then the disc samples were dimple ground to 20μm in thickness. Finally, the disc samples were further thinned by argon ion milling (Gatan) with an incident angle of 6° until perforation occurred. TEM analysis was performed on a JEM 2010 transmission electron microscope operated at 200kV. HREM observations were carried out on JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope operated at 200KV.

Fig. 1 shows the backscattered SEM image of (TiB+TiC+Nd2O3)/Ti composites. There are three kinds of reinforcements: TiB whisker, TiC near equiaxed particle and Nd2O3 spherical particle. Since the atomic weight of Nd2O3 is much bigger than those of TiC and TiB, Nd2O3 reinforcements exhibit white in the backscattered image. It can be observed that Nd2O3 reinforcements exhibit spherical shape with micron-size (marked as Nd2O3-A) and nanometer size (marked as Nd2O3-B).

Fig. 2 shows the TEM bright field picture of Nd2O3 reinforcements in the (TiB+TiC+Nd2O3)/Ti composites. It was found that the nano-sized Na2O3 particles were dispersed inside the titanium grain and some micron-sized Na2O3 particles arrayed along the grain boundary. The elemental surface scanning by energy spectrometer was used to confirm the phase of the particle. The results were shown in Fig. 3 . In the elemental surface scanning image, the color turns from black to white with the element content increasing. Since the atomic number of oxygen is too small, it's difficult for oxygen element to be detected resulting in the whole dark in the Fig. 3(c). The particle sizes are different in Fig. 3(b) and (d), which is caused by the spherical shape of Nd2O3 particle in space.

Nd2O3 particles were in situ formed during solidification [4,6]. The different sizes of Nd2O3 particles attribute to the various formation stages. When cooling, some Nd atoms were solid soluted in β-Ti since Nd atom has a solid solubility of 2% atomic percentage in β-Ti phase. The rest Nd atoms reacted with O atoms leading to formation of primary micron-sized Nd2O3 particles. Since the diffusion of oxygen along the grain boundary is much easier than inside the grain, the micron-sized Nd2O3 particles arrayed along the grain finally. When further cooling, the solid soluted Nd atoms became precipitate from the β-Ti phase and reacted with O atoms resulting in the formation of nano-sized Nd2O3 particles.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the bright field TEM image of Nd2O3 particle in the (TiB+TiC+Nd2O3)/Ti composites. Fig. 4(b)–(d) show the selected area diffraction (SAD) patterns of Nd2O3 along [110] zone axis, α-Ti along
[
1 ¯
2
1 ¯
3 ¯
]
zone axis and Nd2O3–Ti interface, respectively. The SAD patterns reveal the following orientation relationship between Nd2O3 and α-Ti:
[ 1

1 ¯
1 ¯
0 )
Ti
.
The orientation relationship was in good agreement with the simulation results (shown in Fig. 5 ).

Fig. 6 shows the high-resolution transmission microscopy (HREM) image of Nd2O3–Ti interface with corresponding SAD patterns. From the HREM image, it can be found that the interface between Nd2O3 and Ti is very clean and there's no interfacial reaction product. The SAD patterns reveal another set of orientation relationship between Nd2O3 and Ti:
[ 0
0
1 ]
N
d 2
O 3
/ /
[
1 ¯
1
0
1 ]
Ti
;

0
0 )
N

O 3
/ /
(
2 ¯
1
1
3 ¯
)
Ti
.
The parallel planes
( 1
0
0 )
N
d 2
O 3
and
(
2 ¯
1
1
3 ¯
)
Ti
could be directly observed from the HREM image. The orientation relationship is also in good agreement with the simulation results (shown in Fig. 7 ).

The magnified and optimized HREM images of place A and place B in Fig. 6 were shown in Fig. 8 . The internal delicate crystal structures of α-Ti and Nd2O3 can be observed from Fig. 8(a) and (b), respectively. Dislocations were both found in both the Ti matrix and the Nd2O3 particle. The appearance of dislocations may attribute to the mismatching of thermal expansion coefficient between Ti and Nd2O3. When cooling, the mismatching of thermal expansion coefficient between Ti and Nd2O3 can cause internal stress in both Ti matrix and the Nd2O3 particle, which leads to the occurring of dislocations in the crystal.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The Nd2O3 reinforcements in the in situ synthesized (TiB+TiC+Nd2O3)/Ti composites exhibit spherical shape with both micron-size and nano-size. The interface between Nd2O3 and Ti matrix is very clean and there's no reaction product.

The following orientation relationships between Nd2O3 and α-Ti are determined from observed images as follows:

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to acknowledge a financial support provided by a Foundation for the Author of National Excellent Doctoral Dissertation of PR China under Grant No. 200332, Major Fundamental Research Project of Shanghai Science and Technology Committee under Grant Nos. 04DZ14002, 04QMX1412, 0452nm045, Aeronautics Foundation of China under Grant No. 03G57007 and ItoYama Foundation.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Z.F. Yang, W.J. Lu, D. Xu, J.N. Qin, D. Zhang, J. Alloy Compd., in press.
